titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Vic Dickenson Papers
titlestmt.author: by Brooke McManus

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 19, 2025 at 23:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection contains musical scores, correspondence, photographs, musical instruments, jewelry and accessories, personal and professional papers, printed material, audiotapes, and other papers by or relating to African American jazz trombonist Vic Dickenson and his family.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 24 (sound recordings): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Langdon Manor Books, LLC on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2018, 2019.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Vic Dickenson Papers, 1910-1992, undated. II. Dickenson Family Papers, 1870-1966. III. Sound Recordings, 1972-1992, undated.

bioghist.head: Vic Dickenson (1906-1984)
bioghist.p: Arthur Victor "Vic" Dickenson was an African American jazz trombonist born in Xenia, Ohio. Dickenson began his career in the early 1920s when he formed a band with his brother Carlos and moved to Columbus, Ohio. Thereafter he traveled frequently and played with different groups. Dickenson joined Blanche Calloway's orchestra in the early 1930s and played with the Count Basie Orchestra in 1940-1941. In 1943, he joined a sextet led by pianist Eddie Heywood. Dickenson subsequently led his own bands from the late 1940s to the mid-1950s. In 1953, he recorded The Vic Dickenson Showcase for Vanguard with Ed Hall on clarinet and Ruby Braff on trumpet. Dickenson appeared on the CBS television program "The Sound of Jazz" in 1957 with Count Basie, Coleman Hawkins, Roy Eldridge, Gerry Mulligan, and Billie Holiday, and in 1958, he was among the 57 jazz musicians photographed by Art Kane for Esquire magazine's "A Great Day in Harlem" shoot. In the 1950s, Dickenson often performed with Bobby Hackett. He also collaborated with Sidney Bechet, Bucky Clayton, and Pee Wee Russell. In the 1960s, Dickenson was a member of the jazz ensemble Saints and Sinners, and he joined the World's Greatest Jazz Band in the 1970s. In 1982, the Vic Dickenson Quintet was nominated for a Grammy Award for Best Jazz Instrumental Performance, Group.
Dickenson met Otealia Foyer in 1932, and they married four years later. He died in New York in 1984.

bioghist.head: Dickenson Family
bioghist.p: Jane Mayo was Vic Dickenson's great-grandmother. She lived in Catlettsburg, Kentucky and was married to Anderson Nelson until his death in 1871. She remarried in 1875 to Hampton Mayo. Martha Alexander (1847-1919) was Jane Mayo's daughter; her children included Cora, Joe, and Lou Ellyn (1872-1951), Dickenson's mother. Lou Ellyn married Robert Dickenson from Ceredo, West Virginia and settled in Xenia, Ohio, where they had nine children, including Vic, Arthur, Robert, Carlos, and Mae.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Vic Dickenson Papers. James Weldon Johnson Memorial Collection in the Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains musical scores, correspondence, photographs, musical instruments, jewelry and accessories, personal and professional papers, printed material, audiotapes, and other papers by or relating to African American jazz trombonist Vic Dickenson and his family.

Musical scores document Dickenson's collaborations with Bobby Hackett and Lucille Hall. Correspondence primarily consists of postcards and greeting cards from entertainers such as Tommy Benford, Doc Cheatham, Bob Crosby, Bob Haggart, Benny Morton, Gerry Mulligan, Red Richards, Clark Terry, Sir Charles Thompson, and Trummy Young, and authors Stanley Dance and Rosetta Reitz. Correspondence with Dickenson's parents and siblings is also present. The collection contains photographs of musicians Louis Armstrong, Sidney Bechet, Benny Carter, Buck Clayton, Eddie Condon, Bobby Hackett, Charles Mingus, and Pee Wee Russell. Included are scenes from the "A Great Day in Harlem" shoot for Esquire and rehearsals for "The Sound of Jazz," photographed by bassist Milt Hinton. Musical instruments consist of a Wurlitzer trombone, Vincent Bach Stradivarius trumpet, mutes, and a Hohner melodica. Accessories include tie tacks and cufflinks with a trombone design, pin-back buttons relating to jazz festivals, and several felt hats. Personal and professional papers contain notes, writings, drawings, scrapbooks, financial records, certificates, and Dickenson's 1982 Grammy nomination. Printed material consists of fliers, programs, invitations, and other ephemera for performances by Dickenson, Saints and Sinners, and the World's Greatest Jazz Band, as well as magazines relating to jazz. Audiotapes include live recordings of Dickenson, Bobby Hackett, Pee Wee Russell, and other musicians.

Dickenson family papers consist of correspondence, photographs, and financial records documenting the lives of Dickenson's great-grandmother Jane Mayo, grandmother Martha Alexander, and mother Lou Ellyn Dickenson. Photographs include cartes-de-visite, cabinet photographs, and photographic prints dating from the 1880s. A photograph album shows several generations of the Dickenson family, students at Wilberforce Univerity, and Black soldiers in uniform from 1919 to 1921.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Vic Dickenson Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of manuscript and printed music, correspondence, photographs, musical instruments, scrapbooks, ephemera, magazines, and other materials documenting Vic Dickenson's career and personal life.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six subseries: Music, Correspondence, Photographs, Objects, Personal and Professional Papers, and Printed Material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of manuscript and printed music by Dickenson, Bobby Hackett, Lucille Hall, and others, including "Black Bottom Penthouse Serenade," "Constantly," and "Michelle," as well as two music composition notebooks.

did.unittitle: "Alone," music by Dickenson, lyrics by Gwen LuBell
"Canadian Theme," music and lyrics by Doug Willson
"Constantly," music by Dickenson, lyrics by Lucille Hall
"If I Had a Chance," lyrics by Lucille Hall
"I'll Try," music by Dickenson, lyrics by Gwen LuBell
"Mother Nature's Lullaby," lyrics and letter to Dickenson by Lucille Hall
"Once Again in My Arms"/"Nothing to Lose"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note by Dickenson on recto: "From an old Flecher Henderson record sung by June Cole."

did.unittitle: "You Just Gotta Know Where to Go"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists primarily of postcards and greeting cards to Dickenson from members of the jazz community, and Dickenson's correspondence with his family. Correspondents include Tommy Benford, Jack Bradley, Doc Cheatham, Bob Crosby, Stanley Dance, Wilma Dobie, Bob Haggart, Benny Morton, Gerry Mulligan, Rosetta Reitz, Red Richards, Clark Terry, Jesper Thilo, Sir Charles Thompson, and Trummy Young. Early letters from Dickenson to his mother concern his personal life and professional progress as a musician, and several are from Dickenson's bandmate Saunders Richardson in the 1920s. Also included is correspondence of Victorita Thompson, Dickenson's niece, with minister John Garcia Gensel, musicians Jane Jarvis and Ed Polcer, Dickenson biographer Manfred Selchow, and others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent, with unidentified correspondence filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typed letter, signed, regarding performance of Dickenson and The New York Jazz Repertory Company at White House state dinner. Includes dinner program.

did.unittitle: Dance, Stanley, with The Toilet Book
Dobie, Wilma, with copy of Offbeat Jazz newsletter
Young, Trummy (includes photographs of Dickenson and Young performing)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of photographs of Dickenson, friends, family, and other musicians, such as Louis Armstrong, Sidney Bechet, Benny Carter, Buck Clayton, Eddie Condon, Bobby Hackett, Charles Mingus, and Pee Wee Russell. Included are photographs of the "A Great Day in Harlem" shoot, which featured Dickenson and 56 other jazz musicians, and rehearsals for "The Sound of Jazz" television program. Images also depict Dickenson with the jazz ensembles Saints and Sinners and World's Greatest Jazz Band, and various performances in the United States, France, Germany, and Japan.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The collection includes a list by jazz historian Michael Steinman identifying individuals, locations, and events depicted in photographs numbered 1-214. A copy of the document is housed in Box 4, f. 64.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of Dickenson with Bobby Hackett, Saints and Sinners, and the World's Greatest Jazz Band. Many are signed or inscribed.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickenson with friends and other musicians

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains Dickenson's trombone, trumpet, melodica, and mutes, as well as two felt hats, cufflinks, tie tacks, and pin-back buttons from jazz festivals and other events. Dickenson sometimes covered the bell of his trombone with a wool beret, and a beret in the collection may have been used for that purpose.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Not arranged in any particular order

did.unittitle: World's Greatest Jazz Band sew-on patches
Dixieland Jubilee pin-back button and ribbon
Humes & Berg Stonelined Sh! Sh! Quiet Mannie Klein Practice Mute

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains early school materials and artwork, writings, pen and ink drawings, scrapbooks, financial records, and other papers by or relating to Dickenson, including the Vic Dickenson Quintet's 1982 Grammy nomination. Also present are notes dictated by Dickenson to his niece Victorita Thompson regarding his early life, family, career, and fellow musicians; and notes by Thompson on Dickenson's professional life.

did.unittitle: "The Alcoa Hour: The Magic Horn" draft, typescript, corrected

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickenson played a trombonist named Stromberg Harris in the episode.

did.unittitle: Autobiographical notes dictated to Victorita Thompson [notebook and loose notes]
Biographical notes, copy of death certificate
"Birthday party for Vic August 1984 at Eddie Condon's" [messages from friends written on blank music sheet]
Early papers, report cards, and drawings
Festival of Traditional Jazz schedule, annotated by Dickenson
"How Do They Age So Well?" draft, typescript, corrected

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Article about Dickenson and two other musicians by Graham Colombe.

did.unittitle: Ink and pencil drawings by Dickenson in 1910 copy of Walter Scott's Lady of the Lake
Ink and pencil drawings of Dickenson by others

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes signed drawings by Peter Manders and Don Hester.

did.unittitle: Jazz-Club of Schaffhausen, International Trombone Association certificates
La Grande Parade du Jazz (Nice, France) musician information packet
Notes and materials found with sound recordings

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Series III, Sound Recordings, Box 15, f. 141, 143-144.

did.unittitle: Overseas Press Club certificate honoring Dickenson
Vic Dickenson Memorial Jazz Concert tickets and envelope
Vic Dickenson Quintet Grammy nomination for Best Jazz Instrumental Performance, Group
Victor Dickenson and Charles C. [Red] Richards recording contract with Roger Pola

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of fliers, handbills, programs, and other ephemera for early performances by Dickenson and his later appearances with Saints and Sinners and the World's Greatest Jazz Band, as well as clippings and magazines featuring reviews and articles about Dickenson and jazz.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two subheadings: Ephemera and Clippings and Serials. Within subheadings, materials are arranged alphabetically by folder title.

did.unittitle: Fliers, handbills, invitations, programs, and other ephemera

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advertising the group's performance at Le Hot Club de Dijon, France. Poster features an image of Dickenson with a cigarette dangling from his mouth.

did.unittitle: Saints and Sinners, programs, handbills, and fliers
World's Greatest Jazz Band, handbills and fliers

title.part: Bulletin du hot club de France

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence, photographs, and financial records of the Alexander, Dickenson, and Mayo families. The bulk of the correspondence consists of letters from Vic's mother Lou Ellyn to his father Robert and concern their marriage, children, and economic matters. Photographs show Vic Dickenson and his siblings during infancy, childhood, and early adulthood, as well as his parents and other relatives. A family album contains images of the Dickensons at home, at the beach, and during picnics and other outings; students on the campus of Wilberforce University; and group portraits and candid photographs of Black soldiers in military uniforms, who may have served in World War I. Account books of Martha Alexander pertain to the finances of the Catlettsburg AME Zion church.

did.unittitle: Receipts, invoice, notice to vacate (primarily Jane Mayo)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of audio recordings of music performed by Vic Dickenson, Bobby Hackett, and others, as well as a 1992 memorial for Dickenson at the Murphy Theatre in Ohio.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two subheadings: Open Reel Audiotapes and Audiocassettes. Within subheadings, materials are arranged alphabetically by title, with unidentified recordings filed at the end of the sequence.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

did.unittitle: "Bobby Hackett Live at the Roosevelt Grill (Side 1)"

did.unittitle: Recordings from Brew's (jazz club?) sent to Dickenson by Michael Steinman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Included a note to Dickenson: "Dear Vic -- This contains everything that I taped in the four days at Brew's. My best wishes always -- Michael Steinman."

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Series I. Vic Dickenson Papers, Box 9, f. 92.
